items.title: China's rare-earth exports to the US remain critically low: China's monthly exports of rare-earth magnets to the US averaged 479 tons in H1 2026, on track for the lowest annual monthly average since 2021. By comparison, exports averaged 586 tons between 2022 and 2024, ~22% higher than the current rate. These components are critical to industries spanning electric vehicles, industrial automation, and defense, making secure access to them a key priority for US manufacturers. This comes despite October's truce between President Trump and President Xi, in which China pledged to keep rare-earth magnets flowing as a key part of pausing the trade war. China remains the dominant force in the industr
items.content:
China's rare-earth exports to the US remain critically low:

China's monthly exports of rare-earth magnets to the US averaged 479 tons in H1 2026, on track for the lowest annual monthly average since 2021.

By comparison, exports averaged 586 tons between 2022 and 2024, ~22% higher than the current rate.

These components are critical to industries spanning electric vehicles, industrial automation, and defense, making secure access to them a key priority for US manufacturers.

This comes despite October's truce between President Trump and President Xi, in which China pledged to keep rare-earth magnets flowing as a key part of pausing the trade war.

China remains the dominant force in the industry, controlling over 90% of global rare-earth magnet production.

Key shipments of rare-earth magnets from China are constrained.
— The Kobeissi Letter (@KobeissiLetter) Jul 22, 2026

items.title: A smaller piece of every US Dollar earned in the economy is going to workers: The proportion of wages and salaries in US gross domestic income (GDI) is down to ~43%, near the lowest since records began in 1929. Gross domestic income measures the total income earned across the economy, including wages, corporate profits, and investment income. This metric has been in a long-term decline since peaking at ~52% in the 1940s. By comparison, between the 1940s and the 1960s, this percentage never fell below 48%. In other words, a larger proportion of economic income is now flowing to corporate profits and investment income, rather than pay workers. The gap between corporate profits and labor income
items.content:
A smaller piece of every US Dollar earned in the economy is going to workers:

The proportion of wages and salaries in US gross domestic income (GDI) is down to ~43%, near the lowest since records began in 1929.

Gross domestic income measures the total income earned across the economy, including wages, corporate profits, and investment income.

This metric has been in a long-term decline since peaking at ~52% in the 1940s.

By comparison, between the 1940s and the 1960s, this percentage never fell below 48%.

In other words, a larger proportion of economic income is now flowing to corporate profits and investment income, rather than pay workers.

The gap between corporate profits and labor income keeps widening.
— The Kobeissi Letter (@KobeissiLetter) Jul 22, 2026

items.title: China is leading the global nuclear race: China now has 39 nuclear reactors under construction, more than the next 12 countries combined. This is followed by India and Russia, at 8 and 7, respectively, less than half of China's total combined. China also has a much younger pipeline of nuclear projects, with reactors currently under construction averaging just ~2.8 years since work began. By comparison, the global average stands at ~5.8 years, inflated by long-delayed projects, such as India's 22-year construction timeline and Brazil's 16-year timeline. Meanwhile, the US currently has no large commercial nuclear reactors under construction. China is building the world's largest pipeline of nu
items.content:
China is leading the global nuclear race:

China now has 39 nuclear reactors under construction, more than the next 12 countries combined.

This is followed by India and Russia, at 8 and 7, respectively, less than half of China's total combined.

China also has a much younger pipeline of nuclear projects, with reactors currently under construction averaging just ~2.8 years since work began.

By comparison, the global average stands at ~5.8 years, inflated by long-delayed projects, such as India's 22-year construction timeline and Brazil's 16-year timeline.

Meanwhile, the US currently has no large commercial nuclear reactors under construction.

China is building the world's largest pipeline of nuclear reactors.
— The Kobeissi Letter (@KobeissiLetter) Jul 21, 2026

items.title: Bearish bets on US stocks are surging: Short interest in the S&P 500 is up to ~3.7% of its free float, near the highest in data going back to 2010. Short interest in the Russell 3000 is up to ~6.1%, also near an all-time high. Both metrics have steadily increased since the start of 2025. Furthermore, short interest across all NYSE-listed stocks rose to a record 9.0% of shares outstanding in late June. By comparison, this metric peaked at ~5.0% during the 2008 Financial Crisis and ~6.0% during the 2020 pandemic. Conditions for a short-squeeze are rising.
items.content:
Bearish bets on US stocks are surging:

Short interest in the S&P 500 is up to ~3.7% of its free float, near the highest in data going back to 2010.

Short interest in the Russell 3000 is up to ~6.1%, also near an all-time high.

Both metrics have steadily increased since the start of 2025.

Furthermore, short interest across all NYSE-listed stocks rose to a record 9.0% of shares outstanding in late June.

By comparison, this metric peaked at ~5.0% during the 2008 Financial Crisis and ~6.0% during the 2020 pandemic.

Conditions for a short-squeeze are rising.
— The Kobeissi Letter (@KobeissiLetter) Jul 21, 2026

items.title: The cost of labor in the US has never been higher. US average hourly earnings hit $37.64 in June, a record high and up +3.5% over the last year. Even for online stores, a single customer service hire now runs around $4,000 per month. As a result, the global AI customer service market is rapidly expanding. Between 2024 and 20233, the global AI customer service market is expected to grow from $13 billion to $84 billion. This implies a +23.2% annual growth rate with expectations of a -5% decline in customer service employment over the next decade. As labor costs continue rising, the return on investment for AI has become increasingly compelling.
items.content:
The cost of labor in the US has never been higher.

US average hourly earnings hit $37.64 in June, a record high and up +3.5% over the last year.

Even for online stores, a single customer service hire now runs around $4,000 per month.

As a result, the global AI customer service market is rapidly expanding.

Between 2024 and 20233, the global AI customer service market is expected to grow from $13 billion to $84 billion.

This implies a +23.2% annual growth rate with expectations of a -5% decline in customer service employment over the next decade.

As labor costs continue rising, the return on investment for AI has become increasingly compelling.Romain Lapeyre (@Romain_Lapeyre)We’ve raised over $100M to create AI customer support that doesn’t sound like AI slop.

Introducing Gorgias AI Agent 3.0: the first AI customer support that’s better than any human.

How it works 👇

  —  https://x.com/Romain_Lapeyre/status/2079636276530037009 
— The Kobeissi Letter (@KobeissiLetter) Jul 21, 2026

items.title: The AI boom is transforming one of the biggest deflationary forces into a key source of US inflation: Import prices for computer and electronic products surged +7.4% in the first 6 months of 2026, to 77.1 points, the highest since January 2016. This also marks a +8.0% YoY jump, the largest annual increase on record. This represents a historic reversal from a downtrend which persisted for over two decades. Between 2006 and 2019, computer and electronic product import prices fell -30%, as improving technology and manufacturing efficiency consistently pushed costs lower. Now, the AI investment boom is straining manufacturing capacity for semiconductors, servers, and electronic components, as co
items.content:
The AI boom is transforming one of the biggest deflationary forces into a key source of US inflation:

Import prices for computer and electronic products surged +7.4% in the first 6 months of 2026, to 77.1 points, the highest since January 2016.

This also marks a +8.0% YoY jump, the largest annual increase on record.

This represents a historic reversal from a downtrend which persisted for over two decades.

Between 2006 and 2019, computer and electronic product import prices fell -30%, as improving technology and manufacturing efficiency consistently pushed costs lower.

Now, the AI investment boom is straining manufacturing capacity for semiconductors, servers, and electronic components, as companies race to build out AI infrastructure regardless of cost.

The AI revolution has flipped technology from a long-term source of deflation into a major driver of inflation.
— The Kobeissi Letter (@KobeissiLetter) Jul 21, 2026

items.title: The global helium supply chain is tightening: China's helium imports from Qatar have collapsed to near zero, from a peak of ~65 million cubic feet in late-2025, the lowest on record. Imports from Russia have declined -50 million cubic feet over the last 2 quarters, to ~38.5 million cubic feet, the lowest since Q3 2025. The drop comes as the Iran War disrupts exports from Qatar, which supplied ~33% of global helium before the conflict, As a result, spot helium prices for immediate delivery have roughly DOUBLED over the last 4 months. Meanwhile, China has introduced new export controls on helium, despite not being a major producer itself, as the country relies heavily on imports to supply crit
items.content:
The global helium supply chain is tightening:

China's helium imports from Qatar have collapsed to near zero, from a peak of ~65 million cubic feet in late-2025, the lowest on record.

Imports from Russia have declined -50 million cubic feet over the last 2 quarters, to ~38.5 million cubic feet, the lowest since Q3 2025.

The drop comes as the Iran War disrupts exports from Qatar, which supplied ~33% of global helium before the conflict,

As a result, spot helium prices for immediate delivery have roughly DOUBLED over the last 4 months.

Meanwhile, China has introduced new export controls on helium, despite not being a major producer itself, as the country relies heavily on imports to supply critical industries, including semiconductor manufacturing and medical equipment.

China has also been an important transit route for Russian helium into Europe since direct EU imports from Russia were restricted.

Helium supply is being squeezed from multiple directions.
— The Kobeissi Letter (@KobeissiLetter) Jul 21, 2026

items.title: BREAKING: President Trump is preparing to announce 10% tariffs on "dozens of countries" this week, per FT. Details include: 1. These new tariffs come as President Trump's current 10% tariffs expire later this week 2. President Trump's advisors have reportedly warned him against doing so 3. Advisors are reportedly worried about risking the economic shocks of the original trade war ahead of the midterm elections 4. The Trump Admin is also working on other investigations that could grant it the legal authority to propose higher tariffs More tariffs are on the way.
items.content:
BREAKING: President Trump is preparing to announce 10% tariffs on "dozens of countries" this week, per FT.

Details include:

1. These new tariffs come as President Trump's current 10% tariffs expire later this week

2. President Trump's advisors have reportedly warned him against doing so

3. Advisors are reportedly worried about risking the economic shocks of the original trade war ahead of the midterm elections

4. The Trump Admin is also working on other investigations that could grant it the legal authority to propose higher tariffs

More tariffs are on the way.
— The Kobeissi Letter (@KobeissiLetter) Jul 21, 2026
